title: Loan Terms and Collateral: Evidence from the Bilateral Repo Market
abstract: We study secured lending contracts using a proprietary, loan-level database of bilateral repurchase agreements containing groups of simultaneous loans backed by multiple tranches within a securitization. We show that lower-quality loans (i.e., loans backed by lower-rated collateral) have higher margins and spreads. We calibrate a model using collateral asset prices and find that lower-quality loans are riskier despite the higher margins, yet cheaper for the borrower. This finding is consistent with a combination of lender optimism and reaching for yield. We also show that lower-quality loans have longer maturity, consistent with models of rollover concerns with asymmetric information.

article.introduction:
Loans secured by collateral contributeto the financing of critical assets such as real estate and the balance sheets of financial intermediaries. The 2007 financial crisis put a spotlight on nonprice loan terms such as collateral requirements and maturity, as lenders' active adjustment of these terms to reduce their credit exposure contributed to the fragility of our financial system. Recent literature has proposed dynamic models of lending in which borrowers hold illiquid assets and face rollover risk. In these models, lender and borrower beliefs can differ, and lenders' actions reinforce one another, leading to runs and maturity “rat races.”1However, despite these theoretical advances, the question of how fragility arises from lenders' use of nonprice terms remains empirically unresolved. A better answer to this question may prove crucial in understanding the dynamics of the next credit crisis.
Most existing empirical studies on lending terms focus on more traditional loans to borrowers such as nonfinancial firms or individuals, secured by one-of-a-kind, indivisible collateral such as real estate or physical equipment. In these studies, identification is challenging because of unobserved heterogeneity of borrower and collateral. Perhaps as a result, the available evidence is mixed.2Moreover, traditional borrowers' exposure to rollover risk is not comparable to that of financial intermediaries and leveraged investors, which rely critically on short-term funding. Thus, insights from these studies are less directly relevant to the new strand of models.
In this paper, we provide directly relevant evidence by using a novel loan-level data set of uncleared bilateral repurchase agreements that financed the portfolio of a large fixed-income arbitrage fund from mid-2004 to mid-2007.3Unobserved heterogeneity is not a concern because borrower quality is fixed and collateral quality is directly observable. Our data consist of loans with maturity between one day and six months, terms that are negotiated on a security-by-security basis, and collateral of vastly different quality. By contrast, most existing studies of repo use index data (Gorton and Metrick (2012)) or focus on collateral pools with limited maturity or quality variation (Copeland, Martin, and Walker (2014), Krishnamurthy, Nagel, and Orlov (2014), Kotidis and van Horen (2018), Dufour, Marra, and Sangiorgi (2019), Baklanova et al. (2019), Julliard et al. (2019)).
Bilateral repurchase agreements are a unique laboratory for the purpose of understanding the terms of collateralized loans in general. First, repo terms are standardized across contracts, featuring only the most basic components of contracts (i.e., price, margin, and maturity) without complex covenants or options. Second, critically for our empirical design, repo is one of only a few loan types with a high frequency of contracting and identifiable, nonunique collateral. We observe the same borrower and lender enter into repeat contracts on identical collateral as well as simultaneous contracts collateralized by different tranches of the same securitization. The existence of sets of contracts in which variation is suppressed along multiple dimensions enables us to mitigate concerns about unobserved confounding factors because loan quality in this case is determined by terms and collateral alone. Furthermore, since senior tranches are safer collateral than junior tranches by construction within a given securitization, we need not rely on potentially noisy inferred measures of collateral quality.
We begin by presenting some equilibrium stylized facts. Loans backed by lower-quality collateral have higher margin (amount of collateral per dollar loaned) and higher spread, suggesting that they remain riskier despite a higher margin. They also have longer maturity, hinting at a role for rollover concerns for the least liquid collateral.
Next, we discuss our results on margin and spread. Compared to the highest-quality collateral, the lowest-quality collateral has roughly 30 bps higher spread over maturity-matched London Inter-Bank Offered Rate (LIBOR), and 16% higher margin. This finding is consistent with previous literature that shows a positive correlation between spread and margin in responding to various sources of risk both in the triparty repo market (Hu, Pan, and Wang (2021)) and in general secured lending (Rappoport and White (1994), Berger and Udell (1995), Strahan (1999), Benmelech, Garmaise, and Moskowitz (2005), Graham, Li, and Qiu (2008)).
While it is unsurprising that riskier collateral is associated with higher margin requirements, it is not theoretically necessary that loans against riskier collateral be riskier or have a substantially higher spread. This finding is consistent with lenders applying a simple value-at-risk (VaR) criterion (Brunnermeier and Pedersen (2009), O'Brien and Szerszen (2014)). Under such a scenario, conditional on a drop in collateral values that exceeds the VaR threshold, the return variance is more pronounced for worse collateral and hence residual lender risk is higher. However, this finding is contrary to the widespread view that the residual credit risk in collateralized contracts is negligible,4a central building block of models in which collateral is used to overcome belief disagreement or information asymmetry (e.g., Fostel and Geanakoplos (2008), Gorton and Ordoñez (2014)).
Shedding further light on these hypotheses requires a quantitative assessment of loan risk and return. To this end, we build a risk-return benchmark based on our transaction-level data. The benchmark is constructed in two steps. First, we extract the information implied in simultaneous prices of multiple tranches from the same securitization to infer a process for the value of its underlying asset pool. This process thus reflects the beliefs of the market (i.e., of the marginal investor). Second, we use this process to obtain distributions for the collateral asset value and for the return on the repo loan itself. We find that loans backed by collateral of worse quality are indeed riskier. In particular, the haircuts we observe are consistent with a 5% VaR threshold under our benchmark, resulting in substantial residual tail risk and higher lender return variance for lower-quality tranches.
Our results also indicate that worse collateral loans arecheaperfor the borrower on a risk-adjusted basis. We identify both lender- and borrower-side frictions that can explain this unexpected finding. Our main hypothesis is a combination of lender and regulator optimism relative to the market beliefs reflected in collateral prices. Lender optimism leads naturally to undercharging for risk. In addition, optimistic lenders have an incentive to “reach for yield,” that is, to aggressively bet on securities whose regulatory risk assessment is insufficient (Becker and Ivashina (2015)). Alternatively, a borrower equity financing constraint could drive our results. If lending against higher-quality collateral relaxes this constraint (e.g., by enabling higher leverage), the borrower might be willing to pay a premium for such loans.
We find that loans on the lowest-quality collateral are cheapest when the collateral securities are complex or the borrower faces less-sophisticated lenders or regulated lenders. In contrast, we find no evidence that the borrower pays more for higher-leverage or higher-carry loans, other things equal. These results support our main hypothesis of lender and regulator optimism in the run-up to the 2007 financial crisis and challenge a prominent theory of the crisis according to which fragility arises when optimists borrow from pessimists (e.g., Fostel and Geanakoplos (2008)). If the observed collateral values reflect the views of the ultimate owners (i.e., the fund and its peers), our finding implies that optimists borrowed from “greater optimists.”
We now turn our attention to another nonprice loan term: maturity. We find that the lowest-quality collateral is typically financed with loans that are one month longer than the highest-quality collateral. For reference, the median loan maturity in our data set is one month. A plausible explanation for this finding is borrower rollover concerns (Diamond (1991), Brunnermeier and Yogo (2009)). We exploit two unique features of our data to test further implications of this hypothesis.
First, our data mainly consist of two classes of structured finance securities, namely, mortgage-backed securities (MBS) and collateralized debt obligations (CDOs). The extra layers of securitization make the value of the latter much more opaque, creating the possibility of asymmetric information between borrower and lender. Consistent with our hypothesis, we find that the negative correlation between quality and maturity is much stronger for CDOs, compared to MBS. Second, our data enable us to identify refinancing events by matching maturing loans with new loans. Borrowers do not need to demand a long maturity if they have no intention to refinance, and therefore a short maturity should signal an intention to sell. Consistent with this prediction, we show that when a shorter loan maturity is chosen, the fund is more likely to sell the asset at the end of the current contract.
Lenders' maturity preferences are less clear-cut. On the one hand, the higher risk of loans on lower-rated collateral should lead lenders to prefer shorter maturities. This is a common prediction arising from models based on agency, asymmetric information, or creditor coordination frictions and a well-established result in the empirical literature.5On the other hand, in the repo market, rehypothecation might lead lenders to prefer longer maturity for illiquid, lower-quality collateral (Infante (2019), Infante, Press, and Saravay (2020)). Our model shows that longer loans are more expensive, other things equal. This finding suggests that in general lenders should prefer shorter maturities for riskier loans, and thus that the longer maturity is attributable to borrower rollover concerns.
A potential concern with the generalizability of our results is that the data set features a single borrower. To mitigate this concern, below we show that our fund was large and profitable but not exceptional within its peer group. We also show that the repo rates on sample contracts exhibit almost identical time-series behavior as the relevant marketwide reference rate. Taken together, this evidence suggests that our results are not driven entirely by idiosyncratic borrower characteristics. Moreover, our empirical setup examines a rich cross section of collateral and features more than 40 different lenders, including almost all major lenders in the bilateral repo market. Thus, our findings of lender optimism and reaching for yield are unlikely to be driven by the idiosyncratic features of any one lender.
Another strength of our paper is the focus on hedge funds, which among the various types of repo borrowers naturally fit the leveraged investor role envisioned by many theoretical models of fragility. Hedge funds take the most aggressive positions, are subject to the highest haircuts (Dang, Gorton, and Holmström (2013), Julliard et al. (2019)), and played a central role in many recent market turmoil episodes.
Our findings contribute to the empirical understanding of “margin runs.” Gorton and Metrick (2012) show a clear hike in margins in the bilateral market in 2007, with a more pronounced pattern for riskier collateral. In contrast, the reaction of the triparty market was less clear-cut: while margins remained “mostly stable” (Copeland, Martin, and Walker (2014)), certain lenders stopped accepting certain asset classes as collateral altogether (Krishnamurthy, Nagel, and Orlov (2014)). Our findings suggest that the risk of a margin run is greater in the bilateral market, where lenders take significant risk, particularly for riskier collateral, and where changes in their beliefs can have an outsized effect on the credit available for specific assets.
The structure of the paper is as follows. SectionIdescribes the data in detail. SectionIIperforms a basic empirical analysis to establish some stylized facts. SectionIIIdiscusses the implications of our results for theories of haircut and spread with the help of a frictionless benchmark model. SectionIVdiscusses the implications of our results for theories of maturity. SectionVconcludes.
article.I. Data:
Our data consist of the complete portfolio of multiple hedge funds that actively traded fixed-income securities over a period of three years, from 2004 to 2007, when the subprime market collapsed. The hedge funds were under the same management and earned carry by taking leveraged positions in the same structured finance securities. The combined funds held on average about $9 billion in securities, making them among the largest in their category according to the Trading Advisor Selection System (TASS) database.
The funds operated at a time-varying leverage of between 5:1 and 15:1, achieved by borrowing money from the bilateral repo market. Some funds operated at higher leverage, obtained with unsecured lines of credit that were junior to the repo lenders and senior only to the fund investors, making the funds even more similar to each other from the perspective of a repo lender. Accordingly, we do not distinguish these funds explicitly in our exposition but rather treat them as one borrower (“the fund”). However, when our identification strategy relies on identifying sets of loans with a common borrower, we always require that within each set the borrower be the same fund.
The fund's main portfolio holdings were securitized bonds and other structured finance securities backed by various types of collateral and featuring varying degrees of complexity. While the repo loans do not themselves have a credit rating, most of the collateral securities do. Simple securities include bonds backed by pools of real-world loans, such as residential mortgages (MBS) or commercial loans (collateralized loan obligations or CLOs). In the rest of the paper, we refer to these simple securities collectively as “MBS.” Complex securities include CDOs and their variations. These securities are backed by other securitized products such as pass-through MBS and even other CDOs (CDO2$\textrm {CDO}^{2}$). Because of the additional layer(s) of securitization, the value of such securities is substantially more opaque. In the rest of the paper, we refer to complex securities collectively as “CDOs.” Finally, the fund also held a few government bonds, corporate bonds, and preferred shares. These are excluded from the analysis except when explicitly stated.
For each repurchase agreement, the data set contains the name of the lender, start date, end date (which can be “open” occasionally), principal amount, accrued interest, rate, required margin, cash margin, and current margin.6In the repo market, the convention is to express required margin as a “haircut.” If a loan has a 5% haircut, the borrower is required to provide $100 of collateral for a loan of $95.
Information about the collateral includes the CUSIP identifier, the current price (per $100 par value), and the current factor. The current factor is the fraction of the initial par value that is still outstanding. For instance, if 30% of the principal of a security has been prepaid, the current factor is 0.7. The most common reported price sources are Bloomberg quotes, trader quotes, a model, and cost basis, but for a little less than half of the contracts the price is missing. Even with missing prices, when a new agreement is initiated, the market value of the security is implied by the loan amount and the haircut. However, on a given day during a contract's term, or when a loan is rolled over, we do not always observe the market price of the collateral.
Finally, information about the position includes par value, market value (equal to current price × par value × current factor), and the security's accrued coupon interest (by convention, book and market value are reported “clean,” that is, they exclude the interest accrued since the last coupon was paid).
One unique and useful feature of our data is the presence of securities whose relative seniority is well defined. The pool of assets in a securitization conduit is cut into multiple tranches, whose relative seniority is thus determined by construction. Within the same conduit, a higher tranche isalwayssafer than a lower tranche.
However, it is difficult to compare loan terms by tranche across different conduits. To overcome this challenge, we augment our data with contemporaneous credit rating information for each asset. Credit ratings are comparable across securitization vehicles and reflect the time-varying credit quality of the assets. Because ratings are also comparable across rating agencies, we use the worst of three (Moody's, S&P, and Fitch) to maximize the number of complete observations. (Using the best of three does not qualitatively affect our results.) We obtain the credit rating history and other CUSIP-level information (e.g., issue amount, asset class) from multiple sources—from the Fixed Income Securities Database (FISD) database for corporate and government bonds and from Bloomberg for structured finance securities.
Furthermore, for structured finance assets, we supplement our data with conduit-level information from Bloomberg. Specifically, we hand-collect the complete securitization structure of every conduit in our sample, including the 1,406 tranches that appear in our raw data set and another 10,920 tranches that do not. For each tranche, we collect all available information including attachment points and full credit history. We use this information to calculate the level of subordination within the conduit for each tranche in our data set, pinning down the precise degree of relative seniority.
We also collect detailed coupon information from Moody's, including base rate and spread for floating-rate securities. This information is necessary for our quantitative model. In addition, Moody's data contain its own rating histories that largely overlap those reported by Bloomberg. However, the two data sets occasionally disagree on the number and order of the tranches. We reconcile and merge the two sources, recovering 113 additional tranches that were missing from Bloomberg and ratings information for several hundred tranches.
We obtain LIBOR and U.S. risk-free interest rates at various short-term maturities from Federal Reserve Economic Data (FRED). This information is necessary for the calculation of the spread, as explained later in this section. Finally, we obtain fund investors' capital flow and fund returns, as well as general information about the fund, from the TASS database.
The raw data consist of 297,606 daily observations of 16,807 repurchase agreements with 54 lenders, financing 1,590 unique securities (CUSIPs), among which 1,406 structured finance tranches from 905 conduits. We drop 1,797 observations pertaining to reverse repurchase agreements (less than 1%) and nine lenders that had a special relationship with the fund (e.g., the parent company of the fund's sponsor). The cleaned data consist of 269,212 daily observations of 13,688 repurchase agreements with 45 lenders, financing 1,496 unique securities (CUSIPs).
Haircuts vary between 0% and 50%; approximately 16% of the contracts have zero haircut. The median haircut is 5%, and about 11% of contracts have a haircut in the double digits. Rates vary from 0.80% to 6.88%.7Expressed as a spread to the relevant LIBOR rate (discussed in SectionI.E), the spread varies between −285 and 157 bps, with a median of just 4 bps and a standard deviation of 17 bps. Principal amounts of the loans (gross of the posted cash margin) vary between $30,885 and over$700 millionwith a median of $10,463,300. Loans larger than $300 million are observed only with Treasury obligations as collateral, but loans between $100 million and $300 million dollars are not exceptional.
These summary statistics and most other statistics in the paper are calculated by counting each contract as one observation. For instance, a seven-day loan is typically observed five times (excluding weekends). For most purposes, counting repeat observations of the same contract as distinct observations would not be appropriate because the contract terms are fixed for the duration of the loan and can no longer react to new information.
TableIshows the asset class composition of the securities in the data, together with median values for the contract terms for each asset class. Repo spread, haircut, and loan amount covary across asset classes. The covariation between haircut and spread is particularly strong and appears related to asset quality. For instance, repos collateralized by preferred shares have both the highest median spread (24 bps) and haircut (15%), whereas Treasury repos have the lowest (−19 bps and 0%). In fact, the haircut on Treasury obligations is identically 0% throughout the sample. In contrast, maturity does not show any pattern across asset classes, except that repos secured by Treasury obligations are shorter.
Haircut and spread also covarywithineach asset class, and once again, securities of lower quality tend to have both a higher haircut and a higher rate. Average and typical haircuts for each coarse rating class in our sample closely resemble those shown in table 1 of Dang, Gorton, and Holmström (2013). This similarity supports the validity of our analysis, which is based on data from a single fund. Dang, Gorton, and Holmström (2013) report that bilateral repo contracts against asset-backed securities from a sample of hedge-fund borrowers have average haircuts of 3% (AAA) to 25% (BB) during the same period. Repo contracts in our sample against the same asset class show a haircut range from 4% (AAA) to 23% (BB).
As an example, TableIIreports selected variables for five repurchase agreements initiated on the same day with the same lender and secured by five different tranches of the same conduit. Within this controlled group, spread and haircut increase dramatically as asset quality drops. Moreover, in this controlled setting it is also possible to see that lower-quality assets are financed with longer-term loans—something that is not obvious from TableI. Our formal analysis in SectionsII.,III.toII.,IV.expands on this example.
Regardless of whether individual collateral items are related to one another, repo parties often have many simultaneous contracts as in TableII. For this reason, it is customary for repo parties to stipulate a netting agreement such that, in case of borrower bankruptcy, the entire collateral would be applied to satisfy all of the lender's claims. For instance, suppose that on the day of default the D tranche is wiped out, and therefore the lender is owed the entire principal ($3.4m). At the same time, the A1 tranche drops by only 1%. After liquidating it, the lender has113.7/(1−4%)×(1−1%)=$113.7 / (1-4\%) \times (1 - 1\%) =$$117.3m cash, that is, $3.6m more than necessary to pay the debt. Pursuant to the netting agreement, the lender would net the two positions and return $0.2m to the borrower's estate.
In light of this convention, the fact that the parties agreed upon (and therefore we are able to observe) separate terms for each security may appear puzzling. Why do all loans in a set not have the same terms? One important reason is that the risk of each collateral security is different. If all loans had the same spread and haircut, there would be cross-subsidization (i.e., some contracts would be too cheap and some would be too expensive). Since each contract can terminate individually,8all surviving contracts would have to be renegotiated each time. In SectionIof theInternet Appendix, we argue that there is little evidence of cross-subsidization by showing that margins and spreads between stand-alone loans and similar loans that are part of a set are comparable.9Any cross-subsidization, however, would bias our estimates toward zero.
Another apparently puzzling aspect of these sets of loans is their very existence, that is, the fact that one investor would want to hold most of the tranches of a securitization. Carving a pool of relatively homogeneous collateral into high-risk and low-risk securities adds value in the presence of market segmentation, that is, if low-risk securities appeal to certain investors and high-risk securities to others. Our hedge fund, however, engaged in a form of capital structure arbitrage, which required it to hold multiple (possibly all) tranches of the same securitization at the same time.
TableIIIcompares several statistics of the fund with those of contemporaneous and comparable hedge funds reported in the TASS database. The statistics are based on a sample period from mid-2004 to mid-2007. Our fund is classified as “fixed income arbitrage” by TASS, and therefore as a first comparison group we use all 1,565 funds in this category (Row 1 in the table). Relative to its peers, our fund is large (75thto 95thpercentile of assets) and consistently profitable (5thto 25thpercentile of volatility and 75thto 95thpercentile of Sharpe Ratio), but otherwise unremarkable (25thto 75thpercentile of annualized return and leverage).
As a second comparison group, we use 1,075 funds in the same category that use leverage (Row 2). This group is more relevant since our fund's strategy relies heavily on leverage. The results are qualitatively identical to Row 1, supporting our conclusion that our fund does not exhibit any extreme characteristics in the landscape of comparable funds.
Figure1plots the rates paid by the fund on AAA and BBB collateral as well as the general collateral financing (GCF) rate for MBS collateral.10The observed rates follow a remarkably similar time-series pattern as the reference rate, suggesting that idiosyncratic borrower characteristics do not play a major role in determining rates in our sample. Compared to the GCF overnight rate, the fund consistently pays a roughly 10 bp spread for AAA collateral, likely due to a combination of term spread, risk spread, and markup over the overnight wholesale rate. Repo rates on BBB collateral exhibit wider variation, likely due to the more complex features of the collateral and the potential for disagreement on its exact value among lenders. Interestingly, while the GCF series shows an isolated marked spike, the actual funding conditions of our fund appear smoother.
The interest rate on a repurchase agreement (the repo rate) consists of a reference rate plus a spread. We define the spread as the difference between the repo rate and LIBOR with matching maturity, consistent with institutional practices during our sample period. The resulting spread is therefore net of the term structure of the base rate and of macro interest rate variation, leaving the component that is under the control of the contracting parties. LIBOR is the required return on short-term unsecured loans (euro deposits) to creditworthy international banks. Therefore, it is a low-risk, but not risk-free, rate. Our results hold qualitatively for other reasonable reference rates (e.g., maturity-matched U.S. Treasury bill rates).
Empirically, we can observe the reference rate only at several points in the term structure (overnight, one month, etc.) as opposed to the repo contract term, which can be any number of days. We address this issue by interpolating the term structure using a cubic spline. Our results do not depend on the choice of an interpolating function.
On an average day, the spread of the repo rate over LIBOR is positive (6 bps,t-statistic = 19.5), even though repo is secured, whereas LIBOR is associated with unsecured loans. For reference, the spread over a maturity-matched U.S. Treasury bill rate is 33 bps (t-statistic = 44.5). This evidence suggests that repurchase agreements are priced as if they are risky, contrary to the widespread belief that they are nearly risk-free. While our sample contains a good number of AAA-rated tranches, it also contains many lower-seniority ones, and thus it is not representative of the market at large, which is dominated by Treasury obligations and other high-quality liquid collateral. Nonetheless, the existence of our sample in and of itself demonstrates that repurchase agreements can be used—and were used as early as 2004—to finance risky and illiquid collateral.
article.II. Collateral Quality and Contract Term:
In this section, we present some stylized facts on the relation between collateral quality and contract terms. To do so, we focus on sets of loans between the same lender and borrower against different tranches of the same securitization, within which loan quality is determined solely by the collateral asset and relative collateral quality is measured without noise.
In a frictionless world, collateral quality need not have any predictable relation to margin, spread, or maturity. Given a collateral asset, a borrower can pick any margin or maturity they prefer, and for every combination of terms there is a competitively priced corresponding spread. In contrast, the data clearly show that all three terms rise as collateral quality drops. These patterns hint at the existence of important frictions.
TableIIshows five repurchase agreements initiated on the same day with the same lender and collateralized by five different tranches of the same securitization conduit. Situations such as that in TableIIare not rare in our sample: we identify 3,392 instances in which multiple securities issued by the same issuer are pledged as collateral for multiple loans initiated on the same day with the same lender. Most instances are tranches of the same securitization (MBS or CDO). This setup enables us to obtain a particularly clean measurement of the covariation between loan terms and collateral quality, as shown in Figure2.
Five contracts are represented in Figure2(C1, …C5). The first three contracts (C1,C2,C3) are secured by three tranches (A, B, E) of a conduit; their purchase is financed by loans initiated at timetwith Lender 1. The last two contracts (C4,C5) are secured by two tranches (A1, A2) of another conduit; their purchase is financed by loans initiated at timeswith Lender 2. Next to the name of each tranche, in parentheses, is the tranche's credit rating (AAA, AA, BB+, etc.). This approach has the unique advantage of avoiding measurement problems: collateral quality does not have to be inferred because it is directly observable. Moreover, by focusing on variationwithineach group of contracts, we are holding everything else constant, including the borrower's creditworthiness (because the loans are simultaneous), all relevant lender characteristics, and even information about the conduit's assets that is not reflected in the credit rating.
The vectorRating$\bm {Rating}$contains coarse credit rating indicator variables:AA$AA$,A,BBB$BBB$, and<BBB$<BBB$(below BBB);AAA$AAA$is the omitted category. These indicator variables are constructed using the least favorable current collateral rating as of the contract's initiation date by S&P, Moody's, or Fitch. For instance,AA$AA$is equal to one if the worst rating across the three agencies is AA+/Aa1, AA/Aa2, or AA−/Aa3, and to zero otherwise.13The regression only uses contracts for which at least one rating exists.
The optional vectorQuality$\bm {Quality}$contains proxies for aspects of collateral quality that are not necessarily captured by credit ratings:Volatility$Volatility$,IssueSize$Issue\ Size$, and%Subordinated$\%\ Subordinated$. These variables are included as controls, but also because they are interesting per se.
Our proxy for price risk,Volatility$Volatility$, is asset price volatility as measured by the hedge fund itself over the past month. Because volatility is persistent, past volatility should contain information about future volatility. Higher price volatility implies a higher probability that the collateral asset value will not be sufficient to make the lender whole in case of borrower default.
Our proxy for ease of liquidation,IssueSize$Issue\ Size$, is the security's initial issue amount as reported by Bloomberg. Because in our sample this value ranges from less than $1 million to more than $1 billion, we use the natural logarithm. Securities with large initial issue amounts are likely to be held or at least known by a larger number of investors and to be traded more often, and hence are likely to be liquidated with lower search costs and price impact. Similar reasoning underlies the redeployability measure used in Benmelech and Bergman (2009), who point out that assets that are more widely held can also be sold more easily. More direct measures of liquidity such as bid-ask spread or trading volume were not available. Moreover, such measures are not necessarily better proxies for ease of liquidating the asset conditional on borrower default.
Our proxy for seniority of the collateral tranche,%Subordinated$\%\ Subordinated$, is calculated as the value of tranches subordinated to the tranche in question as a fraction of the total value of all tranches combined. For example, suppose the total value of a conduit is 100 and there exist A and B tranches with values of 80 and 20, respectively. In this case, for the A tranche%Subordinated$\%\ Subordinated$is 20%, indicating that this tranche will suffer losses only after the conduit has lost 20% of its value. Ratings may not fully reflect this information because, first, they are discrete and, second, subordination is just one of several factors rating agencies take into account.
TablesIV,V, andVIcontain three sets of regressions based on equation (1), each with a different contract term (Spread$Spread$,Haircut$Haircut$, andMaturity$Maturity$) as dependent variable. For each table, we run five versions of the specification. Columns (1) and (2) include only tranche indicator variables (Quality=⌀$\bm {Quality} = \varnothing$). Column (1) is a pooled OLS and column (2) uses tranche set fixed effects. Columns (3) to (5) include the quality variables (Quality$\bm {Quality}$= {Volatility,Issue Size,% Subordinated}). Columns (4) and (5) are restricted to two subsamples, MBS and CDO, respectively. CDO tranches are much more opaque and hard to value than MBS tranches, and thus for a given credit rating they have greater potential for information asymmetry between borrower and lender. Finally, the sixth column (titled “Corporate”) reports results from estimating the pooled OLS specification of column (1) on a sample of corporate bonds. Given the small sample size, the corporate bond results are reported only for reference.
By way of preview, Figure3plots the coefficients on the tranche indicators from the baseline fixed-effects specification (column (2)). ForSpread$Spread$andHaircut$Haircut$, a higher value on the vertical axis corresponds to stricter contract terms. Conversely, forMaturity$Maturity$, a higher value indicates more lenient contract terms. The following subsections describe the results for each term.
TableIVpresents the estimates based on equation (1) with spread as the dependent variable. Loan spread, or price, appears to be a function of collateral credit rating alone, increasing monotonically with the credit risk of the underlying asset. The spread on tranches rated below BBB is 21 bps above that of AAA-rated tranches in the full sample, pooled OLS regression (column (1)). Using tranche set fixed effects, this number rises to 31 to 35 bps (columns (2) and (3)), indicating that ignoring unobserved heterogeneity leads to significant underestimation of the coefficient. This result holds without (column (2)) or with (column (3)) the inclusion of quality variables. Controlling for credit rating, the coefficients on the other quality variables are mostly statistically insignificant and consistently very small.
The fact that loans backed by lower-quality collateral have higher interest rates may sound intuitive. However, without any frictions, loan risk need not be correlated with collateral risk. For instance, the lender can adjust margin to target a constant risk level throughout its loan portfolio. This does not seem to be the case: lenders appear to take more risk and receive more compensation when collateral quality is lower.
Comparing columns (4) and (5) (MBS and CDO subsamples), the pricing is remarkably consistent across different types of structured finance securities.14Interestingly, the biggest jump in price is between A and BBB (a 16 bps to 19 bps difference), both for MBS tranches and CDO tranches. This pattern is not consistent with the pricing of loans backed by corporate bonds, for which the AAA/BBB difference is estimated as zero and is insignificant. Corporate bonds show a small 3 bp jump at the traditional investment grade boundary (BBB/<BBB). Thanks to work published since the subprime financial crisis, it is well known that credit rating agencies use the same scale inconsistently for different asset classes (Frank (2008), Cornaggia, Cornaggia, and Hund (2017)), and that structured finance securities were designed to experience extreme negative returns conditional on default (Jurek and Stafford (2011)). This evidence suggests that in the three years before the crisis, the lenders in our data set (i.e., broker-dealers and banks) already perceived structured finance securities as much riskier than corporate bonds of the same rating.
TableVreports the estimates based on equation (1) with haircut (margin) as the dependent variable. The haircut on tranches rated below BBB is roughly 16 percentage points above the haircut on AAA-rated tranches.15
The results for margin are similar to those for spread in a number of ways. First, like spread, margin is a monotonically increasing function of collateral credit risk across all structured finance specifications (columns (1) to (5)). Second, the full-sample pooled estimates (column (1)) appear biased downward compared to their fixed-effects counterparts, both excluding quality variables (column (2)) and including them (column (3)). Third, the results for the MBS (column (4)) and CDO (column (5)) subsamples are similar to each other as well as to the full-sample results. Fourth, these results differ considerably from the results for the reference sample of corporate bonds, for which the AAA/BBB difference is only 4 percentage points. There is a large jump in haircuts between A and BBB in both the MBS and CDO subsamples, but only a small increase (at the investment-grade boundary) for corporate bonds.
This correlation between margin and spread is well documented, both in the triparty repo market (Hu, Pan, and Wang (2021)) and in general secured lending (Rappoport and White (1994), Berger and Udell (1995), Strahan (1999), Benmelech, Garmaise, and Moskowitz (2005), Graham, Li, and Qiu (2008)). However, the correlation that we uncover is surprisingly strong. The response of margin and spread to a decrease in collateral quality is similar in terms of not only the direction but also the functional shape, as is clearly visible in Figure3.
This clear pattern is not predicted by the frictionless benchmark. A similar result can be obtained, however, if lenders set haircuts according to a crude VaR criterion, so that the margin covers the largest possible drop in collateral value with a certain degree of confidence π, as in Brunnermeier and Pedersen (2009). Under such a criterion, riskier assets have larger margins so as to hold π constant. However,conditional on a drop in collateral value greater than the margin, a riskier asset also causes the lender to suffer a greater expected loss. Therefore, riskier assets cause a larger expected loss with the same probability π, and a higher spread is required to compensate the lender for the larger expected loss.
If lower-quality collateral loans are riskier, it is natural to ask whether lenders' risk compensation is uniform across loans with heterogeneous risk levels as would be the case in a frictionless world. The answer to this question can shed light on the most important underlying frictions. For instance, riskier loans could be more “expensive” (i.e., have higher compensation per unit of risk) in a belief-disagreement model like Fostel and Geanakoplos (2008) in which borrowers-buyers (henceforth, “the market”) are relatively optimistic and lenders are relatively pessimistic.16In such a model, taken literally, collateral is used to make loans risk-free. Our results in TablesIVandValready rule out this possibility. However, under a more general approach to belief disagreement, the absence of high-risk repos is replaced by expensive high-risk repos.17In SectionIII, we empirically test this broader class of belief-disagreement models by directly estimating loan expensiveness.
Riskier loans could also be cheaper if, for instance,lendersare more optimistic than borrowers. Loans on lower-quality collateral are more affected by belief differences, and thus optimistic lenders would naturally undercharge more for riskier loans. In addition, optimistic lenders constrained by regulation could have an incentive to reach for yield (Becker and Ivashina (2015)). If regulators set the risk weights too low for lower-quality collateral, lending against such collateral would enable the lender to take more riskper unit of regulatory capital. To achieve this goal, a competitive lender might be willing to accept somewhat lower risk compensation yet still expect a higher total return. A combination of lender optimism and reaching for yield is consistent with anecdotal evidence as well as academic literature (Jurek and Stafford (2011), Cornaggia, Cornaggia, and Hund (2017)) suggesting that at the time many were indeed underestimating the risk of the collateral.
Unlike spread, margin is also affected by the quality variables (columns (3) to (5)). Although not all coefficients are statistically significant in all specifications, the coefficients have the expected sign and are economically meaningful. Higher haircuts are associated with collateral that is more volatile, has smaller issue size, and has a less senior position within the securitization. A 1-percentage-point increase in realized volatility is associated with a 0.30 to 0.45 percentage point increase in haircut. A 1% decrease in issue size is associated with a 0.07 to 0.18 percentage point increase in haircut.
Finally, the coefficient on%Subordinated$\%\ Subordinated$in columns (3) to (5) shows that tranche seniority has significant impact on margin. For example, column (3) reports that a 1-percentage-point increase in%Subordinated$\%\ Subordinated$leads to a 0.45 to 0.65 percentage point decrease in haircut. This result indicates that relative seniority within a conduit acts as implicit margin, together with the haircut explicitly set by the loan contract.
TableVIshows the estimates based on equation (1) with maturity as the dependent variable. Existing empirical evidence on maturity choice is mixed. Higher risk or asymmetric information are associated with both shorter maturity (Graham, Li, and Qiu (2008), Magri (2010), Bharath et al. (2011), Custódio, Ferreira, and Laureano (2013)) and longer maturity (Berger et al. (2005), Hertzberg, Liberman, and Paravisini (2016)).18In addition, Benmelech, Garmaise, and Moskowitz (2005) and Benmelech (2009) find that loans backed by easier-to-sell collateral have longer maturity.
In our case, the evidence is clear-cut: riskier loans have longer maturity. Lower credit ratings are associated with longer loan maturity in a statistically significant way, even controlling for other quality variables (columns (1) to (5)). The coefficients onVolatility$Volatility$and%Subordinated$\%\ Subordinated$are not statistically significant, but their signs go in the same direction in all specifications: loans backed by more volatile and less senior collateral have longer maturities. A natural explanation for this finding is borrower rollover concerns. The borrower trades off the stability of long-term debt—for example, because the collateral is illiquid and a rollover failure would result in a costly fire-sale—and the financial flexibility of short-term debt, which can mitigate asymmetric information problems (Diamond (1991), Brunnermeier and Yogo (2009)).
The magnitudes of the coefficients on ratings indicator variables depend on the specification. In column (1), the pooled OLS specification, loans backed by tranches rated below BBB have maturity about 17 days longer than loans backed by AAA-rated tranches. However, in column (2), the fixed-effects specification, the difference is only seven days. In this case, the OLS regression is more reliable. The difference is due to the fact that in many cases, when multiple tranches of the same securitization are pledged as collateral on the same day with the same lender, all loans are constrained to have the same maturity for operational convenience. This friction artificially suppresses variation and biases the coefficients toward zero. (In SectionIof theInternet Appendix, we show that loans that are part of sets have slightly longer maturity than loans that are not. In SectionIIof theInternet Appendix, we estimate the same specifications as in columns (2) to (5) using only the tranche sets in which maturity is not constant, and we recover a coefficient similar to the OLS estimate.)
Furthermore, the results in columns (4) and (5) of TableVIsupport the view that asymmetric information plays an important role in determining maturity choice. The variation of loan maturity over collateral quality is much larger for CDO tranches (column (5)) than for MBS tranches (column (4)), which are much more transparent and easy to value. This evidence is most compatible with Diamond (1991): the additional opacity of CDOs makes it difficult for positive information to be reflected in prices and thus weakens the option value of refinancing with a cheaper loan. This induces the borrower to choose longer maturities for more illiquid securities. The results are also compatible with the theory of Brunnermeier and Yogo (2009): if CDO tranches are harder to value, the risk of not being able to refinance (i.e., not finding another lender willing to accept the collateral) is higher, tilting the borrower's preference toward a longer maturity for lower-quality tranches.
article.III. Model-Based Results:
In SectionII, we show that the terms of equilibrium contracts display clear patterns, hinting at the existence of underlying frictions, and we propose a few plausible explanations. In this and the next section, we test their implications. Our main challenge is to study jointly determined contract terms individually. One way to sidestep this problem is to focus on one-dimensional outcomes. To this end, in this section we develop a frictionless benchmark to compute loan risk and expected returns and test our hypotheses for the findings on margin and spread. We discuss the maturity results separately in SectionIV.
The model's key ingredients are collateral asset prices. The borrower reports this information to the lender upon the initiation of each loan. Furthermore, as discussed above, the borrower occasionally initiates multiple loans backed by sibling tranches from the same MBS or CDO securitization vehicle. Thanks to this unique feature of our data, in these instances we observe contemporaneous prices of multiple tranches, which we use to approximately characterize the distribution of future prices. Using a set of criteria outlined in SectionIIIof theInternet Appendix, we identify 306 suitable tranche sets accounting for 896 actual contracts.
For each securitization and every point on the parameter grid, we run 5,000 Monte Carlo iterations to choose the model parameters that achieve the closest possible fit to the observed tranche prices (i.e., minimize the Euclidean distance between model-based and actual prices).
In the second step, we use the fitted process to construct a return distribution for each collateral security at the time of repo maturity. We simulate the fitted process (5,000 iterations) to construct a discretized distribution forλ(d)$\lambda (d)$, the default rate at the time of repo maturityd, expressed in years. For each discrete point of theλ(d)$\lambda (d)$distribution, we then run an additional layer of simulations (5,000 iterations) to determine the corresponding distribution for collateral value,Vd(λ(d))$V_d(\lambda (d))$.
As a result, we obtain a return distribution for each loan contract: the contractual interest (r+s$r+s$), if there is no default, and otherwise a recovery value ofY(1−φ)$Y(1-\varphi )$, where φ is a fire-sale discount parameter. The distribution of loan return is generated for any combination of haircut and spread, thus yielding the lender's haircut-spread indifference curves. For brevity, we take the observed maturity as given. Our base assumptions areω=0.5$\omega =0.5$and, to make our model truly frictionless,φ=0$\varphi = 0$.
Details of this two-step procedure and the underlying assumptions are described in SectionIIIof theInternet Appendix. The appendix also discusses potential model misspecification, providing evidence that it is not a first-order concern, and shows that our results are robust to alternative assumptions for ω and φ.
Using the market price of multiple related derivative securities to infer a stochastic process for their underlying asset is a common technique (Carr and Wu (2003), Bollerslev and Todorov (2011)). To the best of our knowledge, however, our approach—using the recovered information to construct distributions for derivatives on the derivatives—has not been taken before.
We computeHGap$HGap$using two thresholds (5% VaR and 1% VaR) that were industry standards at the time. While 5% is by far the most widely mentioned VaR level in practice, 1% had particular significance for banks. As early as 1996, and all the way through the 2007 crisis, banks claimed to use a 1% VaR as the standard risk measure of trading book daily market exposure for both internal and regulatory capital purposes (Lopez (1999), O'Brien and Szerszen (2014)). A 1% VaR haircut was subsequently adopted as the minimum haircut necessary to claim zero exposure on repurchase agreements under the Basel II Comprehensive Approach.
The definition ofHGap$HGap$gives us a testable implication. Suppose lender beliefs about the stochastic process for collateral values are consistent with the market beliefs implied by collateral prices. Then if lenders set haircuts by targeting a certain VaR level (1% or 5%), our estimates of the averageαL,i,t$\alpha ^{L,i,t}$and of the βs in equation (6) should be roughly zero at that VaR level and exhibit no systematic patterns with respect to collateral quality.
Lenders, however, could set repo haircuts based on a different process (e.g., because they have different beliefs or they face additional risks such as adverse selection). Lenders could also use a criterion other than VaR to set haircuts (e.g., making loans almost risk-free, or equalizing expected loss across loans). If lenders are more conservative in either their beliefs or criteria, we should expectHGap$HGap$to be positive, and vice versa. Furthermore,HGap$HGap$should become more positive for lower-quality collateral if lenders become progressively more conservative, and vice versa. Thus, the variation inHGap$HGap$across collateral quality is informative about lenders' risk management processes.
The first two columns of TableVIIshow the variation inHGap$HGap$across coarse ratings, using the regression specification in equation (6). In column (1), the dependent variable is the 5%HGap$HGap$. The results show no pattern, and no coefficients are statistically different from zero, including the average of the fixed effects (α̂$\hat{\alpha }$= 0.231,p-value = 0.524).20Column (2) uses the 1%HGap$HGap$as the dependent variable. Unlike the 5%HGap$HGap$, the 1%HGap$HGap$is progressively more negative with respect to collateral risk. These coefficients are also plotted in the left panel of Figure4.
The finding in column (1) is important because it suggests that financial institutions used something akin to a 5% VaR haircut criterion and because it provides confidence that our model produces sensible results. Next, if lenders use a 5% VaR haircut, a negativeHGap$HGap$is to be expected under the stricter 1% VaR criterion. Moreover, the declining trend ofHGap$HGap$over collateral risk in column (2) reflects the fact that the incremental haircut to maintain a particular VaR level increases with asset risk. This trend is also consistent with belief differences between lenders and borrowers that affect the 1% VaR haircut but have no systematic effect on the 5% VaR haircut. The next subsections discuss one such scenario: disagreement on tail risk.
In column (3), the dependent variable is the model-implied standard deviation of repo returns for the lender (St.Dev$St.Dev$). These coefficients, also shown in the second panel of Figure4, directly indicate that the volatility of loan returns is higher for lower-quality collateral. These coefficients are also plotted in the middle panel of Figure4.
Taken together, these results suggest that the lender is exposed to tail risk conditional on default and to any additional risks that are not reflected in collateral prices. Moreover, loans against riskier collateral are riskier. It is therefore no surprise to observe the increasing pattern of spread with respect to collateral risk in the left panel of Figure3.
Note that whileHGap$HGap$compares the actual haircut against a reasonable but arbitrary reference haircut (e.g., 5% VaR level),RGap$RGap$compares the actual repo rate against the breakeven rate in a competitive market. Thus, although it relies on assumptions about borrower default,RGap$RGap$is open to a more direct interpretation as relative loan expensiveness, compared toHGap$HGap$.
A systematic pattern ofRGap$RGap$with respect to collateral quality could indicate a form of institutional friction or belief disagreement. For instance, if lenders are reaching for yield, we would observe a negativeRGap$RGap$because riskier loans are priced aggressively. Similarly, if lenders had optimistic beliefs relative to those implied by market prices, they would impose looser terms and we would make the same observation. Alternatively, if lenders are pessimistic, we would observe a positiveRGap$RGap$. In addition, for a given difference in beliefs about the underlying collateral pool,RGap$RGap$on lower-rated tranches would be more extreme because these tranches are typically more sensitive to such a difference.
Column (4) of TableVIIshows the regression estimates for equation (6) withRGap$RGap$as the dependent variable. The results exhibit a clear direction:RGap$RGap$is monotonically lower for riskier collateral. These coefficients are also plotted in the right panel of Figure4. This surprising finding indicates that lower-quality loans are cheaper: lenders earn lower compensation per unit of risk, even before considering potential components of lender risk that are not reflected in the cross section of collateral prices. The rest of the section tries to shed more light on this result.
We believe that a combination of lender optimism and reaching for yield is the most plausible explanation for the pattern we observe. We thus test two predictions that follow from this theory. First, we examine the hypothesis that lenders are optimistic. We focus on a specific form of optimism whereby lenders underestimate the tail risk of the underlying collateral pool. This form of optimism is both well documented in the empirical literature (Heitfield (2009), Coval, Jurek, and Stafford (2009), Foote, Gerardi, and Willen (2012)) and compatible with the strong restrictions imposed by our5%HGap$5\%\ HGap$results (TableVII, column (1)).22The effect of such belief difference would be concentrated in lower-rated tranches and thus we expect optimistic lenders to offer more aggressive terms for lower-rated collateral, resulting inRGap$RGap$progressively more negative with respect to collateral risk.
Our first prediction is that loans collateralized by CDO tranches, as opposed to MBS, should create greater opportunities for optimism as defined above. It is generally understood that CDO securities were more opaque and their returns more fat tailed. In particular, Foote, Gerardi, and Willen (2012) explain that MBS pricing at the time was done via explicit loan-level models in which a decrease in house prices would result in widespread defaults. In CDO pricing, however, the same type of analysis was not performed and the systematic risk factor was not taken into account. To test our prediction, we compare loans on CDO securities (“Treated” group) to the rest. We interact theTreated$Treated$indicator variable withRating$Rating$in equation (6) and show the estimation results in column (5) of TableVII. The interaction coefficients, which capture the difference between the two sets of loans, indicate that CDO-backed loans have significantly worseRGap$RGap$. This result is consistent with the well-documented market practices discussed above. Moreover, the difference in optimism that we estimate helps validate our model, which is agnostic about whether securities are CDO or MBS.
Our second prediction is that less-sophisticated lenders are more prone to optimism. Our identification of less-sophisticated lenders is data-driven. We observe 251 sets of simultaneous loans between the same borrower and lender collateralized by tranches from the same securitization with different fine ratings within one coarse rating bucket (e.g., A+ and A−). This setup virtually ensures that the lender knows one loan is riskier than the other. Yet in 47% of cases (117 loan sets), higher-rated collateral obtains the same spread and same haircut. Accordingly, we define less-sophisticated lenders as those with an above-average frequency of sets with no variation in spread or haircut.23
Note that in spite of the overall high sophistication of the lenders in our sample, we observe substantial empirical variation in our measure of pricing precision. Among loan sets with variation in haircut, the average difference between highest and lowest is 4.0 percentage points within a coarse rating, which is statistically significant. In one case in which we observe the same pair of securities with two different lenders, the sophisticated lender sets the haircut on the lower-rated security 5 percentage points higher (10% vs. 5%).24We interpret this disparity as the expression of underlying differences across organizations in characteristics that drive investment in risk management and pricing, among other outcomes. Such differences are plausible: as discussed above, Foote, Gerardi, and Willen (2012) document that these differences existed at the time evenwithinthe same organizations, as the risk of MBS and CDOs was assessed by different teams with different methodologies and ultimately different outcomes. If the lenders we classify as less sophisticated invest less in pricing capabilities, they are also more likely to underestimate tail risk for complex securitized tranches.
Because we compare less-sophisticated lenders (“Treated” group) to the rest, we estimate equation (6) with Conduit × Month fixed effects instead of Lender × Conduit × Month fixed effects. We report the results in column (6) of TableVII. Because of the different fixed-effects specification, theTreated$Treated$indicator variable is identified, but it is not statistically significant. The interaction coefficients, which capture the difference between the two groups, indicate that less-sophisticated lenders have a significantly worseRGap$RGap$on the lowest-quality collateral.25
Second, we examine the hypothesis that optimistic lenders constrained by regulation reach for yield. We expect banks, subject to the stricter Basel framework, to offer more aggressive financing for lower-rated collateral compared to broker-dealer lenders subject to the Securities and Exchange Commission's “net capital rule.”26Accordingly, we observe that banks in our sample on average take more low-quality collateral than nonbanks. In particular, collateral from the lowest category (BBB or less) is twice as frequent for banks (10.4% vs. 5.7%,p-value = 0.000). In addition, bank lenders are more active in lending against lower-rated collateral and offer more favorable terms: in each coarse rating bucket, both types of lenders (banks and nonbanks) charge comparable median haircuts but banks charge approximately 10 bps lower spreads across the board. Notably, banks lend a disproportionately larger dollar amount (twice as large) with longer maturity (50% longer) against the lowest-rated collateral, resulting in three times more capital deployed for such collateral. We thus expect the declining pattern ofRGap$RGap$to be steeper with respect to collateral risk in the case of bank lenders.
Once again, we estimate equation (6) with Conduit × Month fixed effects. Column (7) of TableVIIreports the results separately for bank lenders (“Treated”) and nonbank lenders. For all rating classes, bank lenders provide cheaper financing for worse collateral. In particular, the magnitude of the return gap for bank lenders is now more than three times larger than for nonbank lenders for the lowest-quality collateral with the difference highly statistically significant. As in column (6), theTreated$Treated$indicator variable is not statistically significant.
We now examine two alternative hypotheses. First, the negative and declining patterns ofHGap$HGap$andRGap$RGap$with respect to collateral risk shown in TableVIIare also consistent with a borrower-side equity funding constraint. This friction is particularly relevant because our borrower is a hedge fund. If the fund is unable to raise enough equity capital from clients to invest in all of the trading opportunities it identifies, it needs to use more debt capital instead, and should be willing to pay a premium to achieve greater leverage. If the lower haircut achievable on higher-rated collateral relieves such a constraint, our borrower would be willing to accept more expensive terms (higherRGap$RGap$). Note that for the present discussion it does not matter if leverage is desired because of greater risk appetite or superior information.
Our results are shown in TableVIII. In column (1), the explanatory variable is raw leverage, defined as the inverse of the haircut. By construction, zero-haircut repos are dropped. The results show no evidence of a significant relation between haircut (higher leverage) and return gap (higher loan expensiveness).
As is the case for leverage, an equity-constrained borrower should be willing to pay more to earn a higher carry per unit of equity. The negative coefficients in columns (2) to (4) of TableVIIIare inconsistent with this conjecture: a higher carry loan for the borrower is associated with amore negativereturn gap for the lender. The coefficients are also economically small. Using our estimate from column (4), a one-standard-deviation change in adjusted leveraged carry (33 percentage points) is associated with one-sixth of a one-standard-deviation decrease inRGap$RGap$(0.11 percentage points). Similarly, using the estimate from column (2), a one-standard-deviation increase in raw leverage carry is associated with one-fourth of a one-standard-deviation decrease inRGap$RGap$. Taken together, this evidence suggests that borrower equity financing constraints are not the main drivers of our findings.
Our second alternative hypothesis is collateral specialness (Duffie (1996), Bottazzi, Luque, and Páscoa (2012)). Progressively lower-rated tranches are likely in greater demand from short sellers because of both smaller supply and greater disagreement on fundamentals, in which case loans backed by this collateral would be cheaper because competitive lenders could earn greater securities lending fees. Despite anecdotal evidence of strong short-selling demand (e.g., synthetic CDOs), due to data limitations, we cannot directly verify that lending fees on lower-rated collateral were indeed higher.
A collateral specialness hypothesis does not directly predict our results on bank lenders and less-sophisticated lenders from the previous subsection. Such a hypothesis might explain our findings if both treated groups were significantly more active in securities lending than their respective control groups and thus willing to pay a greater premium for special collateral, but we find this explanation implausible. Furthermore, articulating such an explanation would be complicated given the absence of a clear overlap between bank lenders and less-sophisticated lenders in our data.
article.IV. Maturity:
In this section, we examine possible explanations for the finding that riskier loans have longer maturities. As discussed in SectionII.D, within sets of loans collateralized by tranches of a conduit, maturity variation is limited. This fact restricts the scope for additional cross-sectional analysis, including the model of the previous section designed to exploit cross-sectional information. Instead, we focus mainly on dynamic maturity choices that occur when the hedge fund rolls over a position.
The maturity results of SectionIIappear consistent with borrower rollover concerns. While we are unable to directly observe the borrower's rollover decisions, we can test predictions that follow from this hypothesis. A very natural prediction is that the borrower refinances early, prior to maturity (Mian and Santos (2018), Xu (2018)). Unfortunately, this mechanism does not generally apply to repo loans because borrowers typically have no contractual early termination right (see Footnote 8).
We thus test another important implication of the borrower rollover concerns theory: borrowers do not need to demand a long maturity if they have no intention to refinance, and therefore a short maturity should signal an intention to sell. More formally, suppose that the borrower expects to hold the asset, and therefore needs financing, forTdays. The lender is willing to lend with maturity up to τ. Rolling over has a nonzero fixed cost, which could represent risk or actual transaction costs. Also suppose for simplicity thatTis exogenous and that the cost of borrowing is the same regardless of maturity. Then ifτ>=T$\tau >= T$, the borrower will choose maturityM=T$M=T$and sell at the end of the contract. Conversely, ifτ<T$\tau < T$, the borrower will choose maturityM=τ$M=\tau$and roll over to the subsequent contract with maturityM=T−τ$M=T-\tau$. Thus, our prediction is that a shortMsignals an intent to sell, that is,∂Pr(Sale)/∂M<0$\partial Pr(Sale)/\partial M < 0$.
Column (1) of TableIXdisplays the results of the regression specified in equation (11). Column (2) reports the results of a conditional logit regression with position-level fixed effects. This regression is identical to the simple pooled regression specified in equation (11), except that β is measured within the series of successive contracts that finance a position. The regression results confirm the prediction, as β is negative in both specifications.
Columns (3) and (4) of TableIXdisplay the results of the logit regression specified in equation (12). As above, column (3) reports the results of the simple pooled specification and column (4) reports the results of the corresponding fixed-effects specification. In the fixed-effects specification, theβk$\beta _k$coefficients are all consistently negative. Furthermore, for loans against A-quality collateral, shorter maturity is a significant predictor of propensity to sell.
These findings are consistent with the rollover concerns hypothesis. However, another possible explanation is that the borrower wants to synchronize the loan maturity with its selling time for reasons other than rollover risk (e.g., operational convenience). To discriminate between these explanations, we examine partial sales, defined as events in which the face value of the position drops but does not go to zero.27
Under the operational convenience explanation, the borrower wishes to match loan maturity and the timing of liquidation, full or partial. In contrast, under the rollover concerns explanation, the borrower aims to minimize the number of rollover events. In a partial sale, therefore, rollover risk remains and the borrower should still want to secure longer-term funding for the rest.
Columns (5) and (6) of TableIX(“Part Sale”) display the results of the same specifications as columns (2) and (4), with a partial sale as the dependent variable. The results show that short maturity does not predict partial sales, further supporting the hypothesis that rollover concerns rather than operational convenience are the primary driver of maturity choice.
While borrowers want a longer maturity because of rollover concerns, many models with agency, information asymmetry, or creditor coordination frictions predict that lenders prefer shorter maturities for riskier loans (e.g., Leland and Toft (1996), Cheng and Milbradt (2012), He and Xiong (2012), Brunnermeier and Oehmke (2013), He and Milbradt (2016)). Because riskier loans require more frequent lender-borrower interactions, lenders place greater value on the option of not renewing the loan.
In the repo market, however, the opposite result could arise because oflenderrollover concerns. Lenders often rehypothecate collateral to obtain funding or lend it to short sellers (Infante (2019), Infante, Press, and Saravay (2020)). If the collateral is illiquid, the ultimate user may require a longer loan, and the lender in turn would offer a longer loan to the borrower in order to secure the collateral for a longer time. Thus, lender rollover concerns could explain the differential results for CDO tranches.
If longer maturity for riskier loans is driven by lenders' motives, we should expect such loans to be cheaper. Conversely, if they are more expensive, we can conclude that our finding is driven primarily by borrower rollover concerns. Our benchmark model can help discriminate between these two explanations. In SectionIVof theInternet Appendix, we estimate a regression with Lender × Conduit × Month fixed effects similar to equation (6) and find thatRGap$RGap$is higher for loans with longer maturity. Specifically, a 30-day longer loan is 14 bps more expensive (11 bps after controlling for credit ratings). Despite limited maturity variation within loan sets, the coefficient on maturity is significant at the 5% level (10% after controlling for credit ratings). This result further supports our hypothesis that longer maturity for lower-quality collateral is driven by borrower, not lender, rollover concerns.
article.V. Conclusion:
Although repurchase agreements are at the heart of financial markets, a large fraction of repo activity is opaque and out of the sight of regulators and most market participants. Furthermore, although repos are often believed to be nearly riskless for lenders, low-quality collateral is increasingly prevalent. For instance, Ozdenoren, Yuan, and Zhang (2021) show that the fraction of AAA collateral has dropped to 25% in 2020 from 50% in 2009. In this paper, we present new evidence on lender risk-taking behavior in the repo market using a unique data set that contains over 13,000 uncleared bilateral repo loans in the three years immediately prior to the 2007 financial crisis. These loans are between a large hedge fund and essentially all major repo lenders in the market and are collateralized by securities of vastly different qualities.
We find that loans against lower-quality collateral are riskier, in spite of their higher margins, and yet too cheap for the borrower. Our results suggest that these findings are due to a combination of lender optimism and reaching for yield driven by insufficient regulatory risk assessment. We also show that loans against worse collateral havelongermaturities, a finding that we attribute primarily to borrower rollover concerns. Beyond our focus on bilateral repurchase agreements, the majority of our findings do not reflect repo market-specific factors and may help shape a more general theory of secured lending.
Editors: Stefan Nagel, Philip Bond, Amit Seru, and Wei Xiong
